title: Albertus Magnus — Book of Minerals
subtitle: The first systematic Christian treatise on mineralogy — four causes, Sulphur-Quicksilver theory, astrological lapidary, and the sigils of stones

summary.one_line: Albertus Magnus's 13th-century synthesis of Aristotelian mineralogy, Arabic alchemy, and Hermetic lapidary lore — organized through the four causes, the Sulphur-Quicksilver theory of metals, and a ...
summary.scope: Covers all 5 Books: (I) Stones in general — material, efficient, formal causes; (II) Precious stones — alphabetical lapidary, cause of powers, sigils and images; (III) Metals in general — Sulphur-Quicksilver theory, transmutation, places of production; (IV) The seven metals individually; (V) Intermediates (salts, vitriols, alums). Extensive field observations on mining districts of Germany (Goslar, Freiberg, the Rhine, Bohemia).

sources.label: Albertus Magnus — Mineralium Libri V (Opera omnia, ed. Borgnet, Vol. V, Paris 1890)
Albertus Magnus — Book of Minerals, translated by Dorothy Wyckoff, Clarendon Press Oxford 1967
Avicenna — De congelatione et conglutinatione lapidum (trans. Alfred of Sareshel, c. 1200)
Aristotle (pseudo-) — Lapidarium Aristotelis / Liber de lapidibus
Constantine of Africa — Opera (including De gradibus and Letter on Incantations of Costa ben Luca)

body.text: Albert structures his mineralogy through Aristotle's four causes: (1) Material cause — stones are made of Earth and Water, with unctuous moisture as the cement; metals are made of Sulphur and Quicksilver. (2) Efficient cause — a 'mineralizing power' (from Avicenna) transmitted via the stars, using heat and cold as instruments. (3) Formal cause — substantial forms imparted by heavenly influences and element mixture; stones have forms but not souls. (4) Final cause — not discussed separately; form is the final cause for inanimate things. This four-cause framework maps directly to the tetrahedron: material = V₄ (Earth/Water), efficient = V₂ (the acting agent), formal = V₃ (the shaping principle), final = V₁ (the end). The same fourfold structure Albert uses to analyze minerals is the same K₄ that Ñāṇavīra identified in experience and the amplituhedron identified in scattering.

And this, the greatest skill of the alchemists, Hermes teaches in his Secret of Secrets, saying metaphorically: the stone 'gently, with great skill, ascends from earth to heaven, and again descends from heaven to earth. Its nurse is the earth, and the wind carried it in its belly.' For intending to teach the operations of alchemy he says it 'ascends to Heaven' when by roasting and calcination it takes on the properties of Fire: for alchemists mean by calcination the reduction of material to powder by burning and roasting. And the material 'again descends from heaven to earth' when it takes on the properties of Earth by inhumation, for inhumation revives and nourishes what was previously killed by calcination. And when he says that 'the wind carries it in its belly' he means the levigation of the material, raising it to the properties of Air. And there distils and issues from the mouth of the alembic a watery or oily liquor with all the powers of the elements.

Albert defends the 'powers' of stones (medical, magical) against materialist skeptics and against those who ascribe them to souls in stones. His solution: the specific substantial form of each stone IS the cause of its powers — not its constituent elements, not Platonic Ideas, not astrological influences alone. This is exactly the formal cause operating through the specific form. The powers are the 'actions' of the form. In the tetrahedron framework, this means each stone's position in the 36-tattva system (its specific mix of elements and heavenly influences) determines its 'tattva function' — its particular contraction of consciousness into matter. The magnet attracts iron because its form 'is' that relation — just as a tattva IS its specific contraction at its specific location in the body.

Albert adopts the Arab Sulphur-Quicksilver theory: metals are composed of Sulphur (active, male, hot-dry, the 'father') and Quicksilver (passive, female, cold-moist, the 'mother'), with the planets determining the specific form. This maps directly onto the iḍā-piṅgalā duality of the subtle body: piṅgalā (solar, active, right channel) = Sulphur = the 'father' = heat that digests and forms; iḍā (lunar, receptive, left channel) = Quicksilver = the 'mother' = moisture that receives and holds form. Their union in suṣumṇā (the central channel) IS the birth of the completed metal — the lapis, the gold, the body of light. The 1:4:2 breath ratio that forces prāṇa into suṣumṇā is the same operation as the alchemical 'coagulation' of Sulphur and Quicksilver into gold.

Let practitioners of alchemy know that they cannot transmute one form of metal into another, but only make something similar, as when they colour a red [metal] with yellow, so that it may appear to be gold; or whiten it until it is similar to silver. ... Specific forms are not transmuted, unless perhaps they are first reduced to prime matter — the [indeterminate] matter of [all] metals — and then, with the help of art, developed into the specific form of the metal they want. ... For whatever the elemental and celestial powers produce in natural vessels they also produce in artificial vessels, provided the artificial vessels are formed just like the natural ones. And whatever nature produces by the heat of the sun and stars, art also produces by the heat of fire, provided the fire is tempered so as not to be stronger than the self-moving formative power in the metals.

I have seen pure gold formed in the sands of rivers in different countries, and in our own country both in the Rhine and the Elbe. ... I myself have found silver as a sort of vein extending throughout the substance of the stone; and this was somewhat purer, but still had some stony calx mixed with it. And it is found in earth as a sort of vein purer than any found in stone; for in the place called Freiberg — which means 'Free Mountain' — it is sometimes found as soft as a firm mush. ... I myself have seen in Swabia in Germany a stone upon which more than fifty serpents had collected. ... There is at Cologne, in the shrine of the Three Kings, an onyx of large size, having the breadth of a man's hand or more; and on it, upon the material of the onyx stone, which is like a fingernail [in colour], are pictured in pure white the heads of two young men.

Albert includes a complete astrological lapidary (II, iii, 5) listing the 12 Signs of the Zodiac by triplicity, the Northern and Southern constellations, and the seven planets, with specific images to be engraved on specific stones for specific effects. This is the same structure as the later Renaissance 'image magic' of Ficino, Agrippa, and Dee — and it maps directly onto the 36 tattvas: the 12 signs = 12 Kālīs = 12 edges of the star tetrahedron; the 7 planets = 7 cakras = 6 edges + center; the 36 decans = 36 tattvas. The astrological lapidary IS the tattva system in Western astrological language.

Albert's final book on 'intermediates' (media) — substances between stones and metals like salts, vitriols, alums, marchasitas, electrum — forms a bridge between the mineral kingdom and the metallic. These are 'incomplete' substances whose forms are not fully fixed, and therefore are capable of being transmuted. This is the alchemical lapis philosophorum concept in embryonic form: the stone that is no stone, the tattva that includes all tattvas, the 38th principle (Anākhyā) that remains when all 36 tattvas are dissolved. Albert's 'intermediates' are the matter of the alchemical work — what must be dissolved and coagulated to produce the completed lapis.

gaps: Detailed comparison of Albert's four causes with Aristotelian originals
Full extraction of alphabetical lapidary entries with mineral identifications
Detailed comparison of Albert's astrological sigils with Ficino and Agrippa
Comparison with Avicenna's original Sulphur-Quicksilver theory
Full extraction of salt, vitriol, alum, marchasita sections

issues.title: Alphabetical lapidary not fully extracted
issues.description: The full 20-chapter alphabetical lapidary (II, ii) with ~120 stones and their powers has not been extracted passage-by-passage. Only summarized.

issues.title: Comparison with Ficino's astrological medicine needed
issues.description: Albert's medical-lapidary and sigil theory should be compared with Ficino's De Sole and De Vita for continuities in the Western astrological-hermetic tradition.

issues.title: Sulphur-Quicksilver theory vs iḍā-piṅgalā mapping
issues.description: The mapping of Sulphur (male, hot-dry) to piṅgalā and Quicksilver (female, cold-moist) to iḍā should be developed into a full comparative analysis with the Tantraloka's nāḍī theory.

versions.author: ai-assistant
changes: Created RO with commentary passages on key doctrines
Added comparative notes on iḍā-piṅgalā, tattvas, alchemical marriage
Identified convergence points with Gowan's hourglass/grail model

outputs.label: Commentary on Albertus Magnus Book of Minerals with Convergence Analysis

timeline.event: RO created from full-text translation of Book of Minerals
timeline.details: Initial extraction and commentary on key doctrines including four causes, Sulphur-Quicksilver theory, astrological lapidary, and field observations of German mining districts
